skill.name: routeros-sniffer
skill.description: RouterOS packet capture and TZSP streaming for protocol debugging. Use when: capturing packets on RouterOS, setting up /tool/sniffer, streaming live traffic via TZSP, using firewall mangle action=sniff-tzsp, debugging network protocols on MikroTik, receiving TZSP with Wireshark or tshark, saving pcap files from RouterOS, or when the user mentions packet sniffer, TZSP, sniff-tzsp, /tool/sniffer, or packet capture on RouterOS.
skill.summary: RouterOS packet capture and TZSP streaming for protocol debugging.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: This skill contains only documentation markdown files for MikroTik RouterOS packet capture and TZSP streaming. The static analyzer flagged 154 patterns, but all are false positives. The scanner confused markdown code fence backticks with Ruby shell execution, documentation example IPs with hardcoded secrets, and relative markdown links with path traversal. No executable code, no data exfiltration, and no security risk exists.

content.user_title: Capture and analyze network packets on MikroTik RouterOS
content.value_statement: RouterOS lacks standard packet capture tools like tcpdump. This skill provides complete packet capture workflows using built-in RouterOS sniffer and TZSP streaming to Wireshark or tshark on your host machine.
actual_capabilities: Configure RouterOS built-in packet sniffer (/tool/sniffer) with filtering
Stream live packets via TZSP to Wireshark or tshark on a remote host
Use firewall mangle rules with action=sniff-tzsp for targeted per-flow mirroring
Save packet captures to PCAP/PCAPNG files on RouterOS flash storage
Set up TZSP receivers on macOS, Linux, and Windows using Wireshark or tshark
Run packet capture labs with QEMU-based RouterOS CHR instances
limitations: Hardware-offloaded bridge traffic may not be visible to the sniffer
Captured packets in memory expire after 10 minutes unless saved to file or streamed
Free CHR license has a 1 Mbps speed limit that may affect capture performance
PCAPNG format (RouterOS 7.20+) may not be compatible with older analysis tools

use_cases.title: Debug network connectivity issues on MikroTik routers
use_cases.description: Capture live packets on a RouterOS device and stream them to Wireshark on your workstation for real-time protocol analysis without physical access to the router.

use_cases.title: Analyze application traffic flows in a lab environment
use_cases.description: Set up a QEMU-based RouterOS CHR instance with TZSP streaming to capture and inspect DNS, HTTP, or custom protocol traffic in an isolated test environment.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot VoIP or streaming quality issues
use_cases.description: Use firewall mangle rules to mirror specific traffic flows (by port or protocol) to a TZSP receiver for detailed packet-level analysis of real-time media streams.

prompt_templates.title: Basic packet capture on RouterOS
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a packet capture on my MikroTik router to monitor traffic on ether1. I want to stream the packets to Wireshark on my computer at IP 192.168.1.50.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: Quick network debugging with live packet streaming

prompt_templates.title: Filter capture to specific traffic
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to capture only DNS traffic (UDP port 53) from my RouterOS router and save it to a file on the flash storage. Show me the commands and how to retrieve the file.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: Targeted capture with file-based output

prompt_templates.title: Firewall mangle per-flow mirroring
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to mirror all HTTP traffic from a specific host (192.168.88.100) to a TZSP receiver at 10.0.0.5 without affecting other traffic. Set up the mangle rules and show me the tshark command to receive the stream.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: Granular flow mirroring with mangle rules

prompt_templates.title: Full lab setup with CHR and TZSP
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me set up a complete packet capture lab using a RouterOS CHR in QEMU. I need the QEMU boot command with port forwarding, REST API commands to configure the sniffer with TZSP streaming to my host, and the tshark command to receive and decode the packets. Include cleanup commands when done.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: End-to-end lab setup with CHR, TZSP, and automated cleanup

output_examples.input: Configure a live TZSP stream from RouterOS to Wireshark on the host at 192.168.88.10
output: On the RouterOS device, configure the sniffer with streaming enabled to your host IP.
Set filter-interface and filter-ip-protocol to narrow the capture scope.
On the host, open Wireshark and capture on the appropriate interface with filter 'udp port 37008'.
Wireshark will decode the TZSP headers and show the inner Ethernet/IP/TCP layers as if captured locally.

output_examples.input: Capture only ICMP traffic to file and download the PCAP
output: Set the sniffer file-name and filter-ip-protocol=icmp on RouterOS.
Start the capture, wait for traffic, then stop the sniffer.
Download the PCAP file via SCP from the router's flash storage.
Open the file in Wireshark or analyze with tshark -r on the host.

best_practices: Always set filter-stream=yes (default) to prevent the sniffer from capturing its own TZSP output packets, which creates a feedback loop.
Clean up sniffer configuration and mangle rules after debugging to avoid leaving unintended capture rules on the router.
Use firewall mangle sniff-tzsp rules when you need multiple independent captures to different receivers, as /tool/sniffer supports only one streaming target.
anti_patterns: Do not leave sniffer running continuously on production routers, as packet capture consumes CPU and memory resources.
Do not set file-limit higher than available free memory on the router, as this may cause system crashes.
Do not disable filter-stream when using TZSP streaming, as this will cause the sniffer to capture its own output in an infinite loop.

faq.question: Why does RouterOS not have tcpdump like other Linux systems?
faq.answer: RouterOS is a proprietary operating system that does not include standard Linux tools. The built-in /tool/sniffer provides equivalent packet capture functionality with additional features like TZSP streaming.

faq.question: What is TZSP and why is it used for RouterOS captures?
faq.answer: TZSP (TaZmen Sniffer Protocol) is a UDP-based encapsulation protocol that wraps captured Ethernet frames and sends them as UDP datagrams. It allows live streaming of packets from RouterOS to any host running Wireshark or tshark without transferring files.

faq.question: Can I capture packets from multiple interfaces simultaneously?
faq.answer: Yes. Set filter-interface=all to capture on all interfaces, or run multiple firewall mangle sniff-tzsp rules to mirror specific interfaces to different receivers.

faq.question: How long do captured packets stay in memory?
faq.answer: Packets in the sniffer memory buffer are available for 10 minutes. For persistent captures, save to a file on flash storage or use TZSP streaming to a remote receiver.

faq.question: Why is my CHR capture running slowly?
faq.answer: The free CHR license has a 1 Mbps throughput limit. This is a licensing restriction, not a sniffer issue. The 60-day trial license removes this limit.

faq.question: Can I use tcpdump to receive TZSP packets?
faq.answer: tcpdump can capture the raw UDP packets but cannot decode TZSP encapsulation. Use tshark or Wireshark to decode the inner Ethernet frames from TZSP packets.
